documentation:
# Phanterpwa
    Tools for the phanterpwadeveloper framework

## Dependencies installed on setup

    psutil
    tornado
    libsass
    transcrypt
    pillow
    itsdangerous
    pydal
    passlib
    requests-oauthlib

## Requeriments
    Python 3.6+
    Python 3.7 Recommended

## Browser Compatibility
#### The browser needs compatibility with:
    HTML5
    ES6 (Javascript6)

## Web Tecnologies
    jQuery
    Font Awesome

modules.name: phanterpwa.helpers
documentation:
Title: helpers Author: PhanterJR License: MIT Coding: utf-8 In this module are gathered all the TAGs currently used for the creation of HTML5 pages. TAG objects inherited the functionality of their parent class, the XmlConstructor You can import all with. >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import * Or just what you want. >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import (HTML, HEAD, BODY, DIV, SPAN) >>> HTML(HEAD(), BODY(DIV(SPAN())))
The complete list can be obtained with. >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import ALL_TAGS >>> print(ALL_TAGS) ['CONCATENATE', 'XML', 'XCOMMENT', 'AREA', 'BASE', 'COL', 'EMBED', 'HR', 'IMG', 'INPUT', 'LINK', 'META', 'PARAM', 'SOURCE', 'TRACK', 'WBR', 'BR', 'A', 'ABBR', 'ADDRESS', 'ARTICLE', 'ASIDE', 'AUDIO', 'B', 'BDI', 'BDO', 'BLOCKQUOTE', 'BODY', 'BUTTON', 'CANVAS', 'CAPTION', 'CITE', 'CODE', 'COLGROUP', 'DATA', 'DATALIST', 'DD', 'DEL', 'DETAILS', 'DFN', 'DIALOG', 'DIV', 'DL', 'DT', 'EM', 'FIELDSET', 'FIGCAPTION', 'FIGURE', 'FOOTER', 'FORM', 'H1', 'H2', 'H3', 'H4', 'H5', 'H6', 'HEAD', 'HEADER', 'HTML', 'I', 'IFRAME', 'INS', 'KBD', 'LABEL', 'LEGEND', 'LI', 'MAIN', 'MAP', 'MARK', 'METER', 'NAV', 'NOSCRIPT', 'OBJECT', 'OL', 'OPTGROUP', 'OPTION', 'OUTPUT', 'P', 'PICTURE', 'PRE', 'PROGRESS', 'Q', 'RP', 'RT', 'RUBY', 'S', 'SAMP', 'SCRIPT', 'SECTION', 'SELECT', 'SMALL', 'SPAN', 'STRONG', 'STYLE', 'SUB', 'SUMMARY', 'SUP', 'SVG', 'TABLE', 'TBODY', 'TD', 'TEMPLATE', 'TEXTAREA', 'TFOOT', 'TH', 'THEAD', 'TIME', 'TITLE', 'TR', 'U', 'UL', 'VAR', 'VIDEO'] There is also a separate list by categories: NORMAL_TAGS, VOID_TAGS, and SPECIAL_TAGS.

classes.name: phanterpwa.helpers.CONCATENATE
documentation:
With CONCATENATE it is possible to concatenate several objects to create the html. Example: >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import DIV, CONCATENATE >>> my_instance = CONCATENATE( ... DIV("This is a div"), ... "this is a string", ... DIV("this is other div") ... ) >>> print(my_instance)
This is a div
this is a string
this is other div

classes.name: phanterpwa.helpers.XML

methods.name: phanterpwa.helpers.XML.xml
documentation:
With this method a string is converted to html with an optional sanitization (predefined tags, predefined
attributes, escape or not strings), by default sanitization is disabled (False).

With XML it is possible to apply an optional sanitization, it is disabled by default, so that every string will be treated as html. Example: >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import SPAN, DIV, XML >>> instanceDIV = DIV("escaped") >>> print(instanceDIV)
<span>escaped</span>
>>> instanceDIV_with_XML = DIV(XML("not escaped")) >>> print(instanceDIV_with_XML)
not escaped
>>> instanceSPAN = SPAN("I will destroy you.") >>> print(instanceSPAN) <evil>I will destroy you.</evil> >>> print(DIV("", XML("I will destroy you."), ""))
<other_evil>I will destroy you.</other_evil>

classes.name: phanterpwa.helpers.XCOMMENT
documentation:
With XCOMMENT it is possible to create html comments.

Example:
    >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import XCOMMENT
    >>> print(XCOMMENT("thit is a comment"))
    <!--this is a comment-->

functions.name: phanterpwa.tools.interpolate
documentation:
Interpolates by replacing strings in certain delimiters

```
param xstring

String that will be interpolated

param context

Dictionary containing the strings that will be replaced, the keys will be tracked in the string and
the values will be replaced by the match found.

param delimiters

Is the pair of strings that delimit each variable contained in xstring

param ignore_non_strings

By default, all xstring will be converted to str, if set to True, different string
values or XmlConstructor will not be changed.
```

Example:
: ```pycon
  >>> from phanterpwa.tools import interpolate
  >>> r = interpolate(
  ...     "long string: Ford {{model1}}, Chevrolet {{model2}}.",
  ...     {"model1": "Focus", "model2": "Celta"}
  ... )
  >>> print(r)
  long string: Ford Focus, Chevrolet Celta.
  ```

functions.name: phanterpwa.tools.sass_change_vars
documentation:
This function is used to change the values of variables in a sass file

```
param string_sass

String with the sass code

param context

The dictionary keys in context represent the variables that will be searched for in the sass code
with their respective new values.
```

Example:
: ```pycon
  >>> from phanterpwa.tools import sass_change_vars
  >>> sass_str = '''
  ... $BG: red
  ... $FG: green
  ... .my_class
  ...     background-color: $BG
  ...     color: $FG
  ... '''
  >>> print(sass_change_vars(sass_str, {"BG": blue, "FG": "black"}))
  $BG: blue
  $FG: black
  .my_class
      background-color: $BG
      color: $FG
  ```

functions.name: phanterpwa.tools.sass_map_vars
documentation:
Returns a dictionary with all the variables found with their respective values.

```
param string_sass

String with the sass code
```

Example:
: ```pycon
  >>> from phanterpwa.tools import sass_map_vars
  >>> sass_str = '''
  ... $BG: red
  ... $FG: green
  ... .my_class
  ...     background-color: $BG
  ...     color: $FG
  ... '''
  >>> sass_map_vars(sass_str)
  {'BG': 'red', 'FG': 'green'}
  ```

functions.name: phanterpwa.tools.generate_activation_code
documentation:
Create a numeric code of predefined size with one character validator.

```
param size

generated string number size.
```

Example:
: ```pycon
  >>> from phanterpwa.tools import generate_activation_code
  >>> generate_activation_code()
  '425938-E'
  >>> generate_activation_code()
  '762093-A'
  >>> generate_activation_code(4)
  '3624-G'
  ```

functions.name: phanterpwa.tools.check_activation_code
documentation:
Checks whether the string in the code parameter is a valid activation code

```
param size

Activation code size ignoring the check digit
```

Example:
: ```pycon
  >>> from phanterpwa.tools import (generate_activation_code, check_activation_code)
  >>> generate_activation_code()
  '425938-E'
  >>> check_activation_code('425938-E')
  '425938-E'
  >>> check_activation_code('INVALID_CODE')
  >>> print(check_activation_code('INVALID_CODE'))
  None
  >>> generate_activation_code(4)
  '3624-G'
  >>> check_activation_code('3624-G')
  >>> print(check_activation_code('3624-G'))
  None
  >>> check_activation_code('3624-G', 4)
  '3624-G'
  ```

functions.name: phanterpwa.tools.config
documentation:
With this function it is possible to manipulate configuration files in json format, by default the file name is
config.json

```
param cfg_file

path to the configuration file

param dict_cfg

dictionary with the values you want to add or change.

param rewrite

With this option the current file will be overwritten with the new settings added in the dict_cfg
parameter
```

Example:
: ```pycon
  >>> from phanterpwa.tools import config
  >>> config("config.json", {"new_key": None})
  {'new_key': None}
  >>> with open("config.json") as f:
  ...     print(f.read())
  ...
  {
      "new_key": null
  }
  >>> config("config.json", {"new_key": False, "other_key": True})
  {'new_key': False, 'other_key': True}
  >>> with open("config.json") as f:
  ...     print(f.read())
  ...
  {
      "new_key": null,
      "other_key": true
  }
  >>> config("config.json", {"more_one_key": "Is it cumulative?"})
  {'new_key': False, 'other_key': True, 'more_one_key': "Is it cumulative?"}
  >>> with open("config.json") as f:
  ...     print(f.read())
  ...
  {
      "new_key": null,
      "other_key": true,
      "more_one_key": "Is it cumulative?"
  }
  >>> config("config.json", {"rewrite": "yes"}, True)
  {'rewrite': 'yes'}
  >>> with open("config.json") as f:
  ...     print(f.read())
  ...
  {
      "rewrite": "yes"
  }
  ```

functions.name: phanterpwa.tools.split_seconds
documentation:
This function converts seconds into a dictionary by splitting seconds without year, month, day, hour, minute
and second when possible.

```
param seconds

seconds that will be converted
```

Example:
: ```pycon
  >>> from phanterpwa.tools import split_seconds
  >>> split_seconds(123456789)
  {'year': 3, 'month': 11, 'day': 3, 'hour': 21, 'minute': 33, 'second': 9}
  >>> split_seconds(121)
  {'minute': 2, 'second': 1}
  >>> split_seconds(3659)
  {'hour': 1, 'second': 59}
  ```

functions.name: phanterpwa.tools.join_seconds
documentation:
Different from split_seconds, this function transforms a dictionary with the year, month, day, hour, minute and
second keys with their respective values in seconds.

```
param splitted_seconds

dictionary with the year, month, day, hour, minute or
second keys.
```

Example:
: ```pycon
  >>> from phanterpwa.tools import join_seconds
  >>> join_seconds({'year': 1, 'hour': 1, 'second': 1})
  31539601
  >>> join_seconds({'year': 1, 'second': 3601})
  31539601
  >>> join_seconds({'year': 1, 'minute': 60, 'second': 1})
  31539601
  ```

functions.name: phanterpwa.tools.humanize_seconds
documentation:
Humanizes seconds by dividing between year, month, day, hour, minute and second when possible.

```
param seconds

seconds that will be humanized

param translator_instance

With this option it is possible to add an i18n instance to translate the return.
```

Example:

: ```pycon
  >>> from phanterpwa.tools import humanize_seconds
  >>> humanize_seconds(123456789)
  '3 years, 11 months, 3 days, 21 hours, 33 minutes and 9 seconds'
  >>> humanize_seconds(121)
  '2 minutes and 1 second'
  {'minute': 2, 'second': 1}
  >>> humanize_seconds(3659)
  '1 hour and 59 seconds'
  ```

  Using phanterpwa.i18n.Translator example

  ```pycon
  >>> from phanterpwa.i18n import Translator
  >>> my_translator = Translator("test_path", "humanize_seconds")
  >>> my_translator.translate("pt-BR", "year", "ano")
  {"pt-BR": {"year": "ano"}}
  >>> my_translator.translate("pt-BR", "years", "anos")
  {"pt-BR": {"years": "anos"}}
  >>> my_translator.translate("pt-BR", "month", "mês")
  {"pt-BR": {"month": "mês"}}
  >>> my_translator.translate("pt-BR", "months", "meses")
  {"pt-BR": {"months": "meses"}}
  >>> my_translator.translate("pt-BR", "day", "dia")
  {"pt-BR": {"day": "dia"}}
  >>> my_translator.translate("pt-BR", "days", "dias")
  {"pt-BR": {"days": "dias"}}
  >>> my_translator.translate("pt-BR", "hour", "hora")
  {"pt-BR": {"hour": "hora"}}
  >>> my_translator.translate("pt-BR", "hours", "horas")
  {"pt-BR": {"hours": "horas"}}
  >>> my_translator.translate("pt-BR", "minute", "minuto")
  {"pt-BR": {"minute": "minuto"}}
  >>> my_translator.translate("pt-BR", "minutes", "minutos")
  {"pt-BR": {"minutes": "minutos"}}
  >>> my_translator.translate("pt-BR", "second", "segundo")
  {"pt-BR": {"second": "segundo"}}
  >>> my_translator.translate("pt-BR", "seconds", "segundos")
  {"pt-BR": {"seconds": "segundos"}}
  >>> my_translator.direct_translation = "pt-BR"
  >>> humanize_seconds(123456789, my_translator)
  '3 anos, 11 meses, 3 dias, 21 horas, 33 minutos and 9 segundo'
  >>> my_translator.direct_translation = "en-US"
  >>> humanize_seconds(123456789, my_translator)
  '3 years, 11 months, 3 days, 21 hours, 33 minutes and 9 seconds'
  >>> humanize_seconds(3659, my_translator)
  '1 hour and 59 seconds'
  >>> my_translator.direct_translation = "pt-BR"
  >>> humanize_seconds(3659, my_translator)
  '1 hora and 59 segundos'
  >>> my_translator.translate("pt-BR", "and ", "e ")
  >>> humanize_seconds(3659, my_translator)
  '1 hora e 59 segundos'
  ```

functions.name: phanterpwa.tools.text_normalize
documentation:
"Remove the graphic accents of Latin languages"

```
param text

string that will be normalized

param upper

This parameter with the value True (default) the output will be capitalized
```

Example:
: ```pycon
  >>> from phanterpwa.tools import text_normalize
  >>> text_normalize("Maçã")
  'MACA'
  >>> text_normalize("Maçã", False)
  'Maca'
  ```

functions.name: phanterpwa.tools.string_escape

the content<\div>") '<div>the content<\div>' >>> result = string_escape({ "one": "
one
", "two": ["
two
"], "three": ("
three
", ), "four": {"
four
"}, "five": { "five_one": ["
", ("", "
"), {""}] } }) >>> result["one"] '<div style='test'>one</div>' >>> result["two"] '["<div>two</div>"]' >>> result["three"] '["<div>three</div>"]' >>> result["four"] '['<div>four</div>']' >>> result["five"]["five_one"] '["<br>", ["<input>", "<hr>"], ["<a href='link'></a>"]]' ```

phanterpwa.configer.ProjectConfig.save
documentation:
saves changes made to configuration files
Example:
\>>> import json
\>>> import os
\>>> import phanterpwa
\>>> from phanterpwa.configer import ProjectConfig
\>>> test_folder = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(phanterpwa.\_\_file\_\_), "tests", "test_configer_path", "project01")
\>>> cfg = ProjectConfig(test_folder)
\>>> cfg['PROJECT']['title']
PhanterPWA
\>>> cfg['PROJECT']['title'] = 'PhanterPWA2'
\>>> cfg.save()
\>>> with open(os.path.join(test_folder, "config.json"), encoding="utf-8") as f:
... content = json.load(f)
\>>> content['PROJECT']['title']
PhanterPWA2

Validates and creates a configuration file in json format based on api.ini and app.ini contained in the project's
applications.

To start when instantiating, enter the path to the file.

```
param dir_or_config_file

Project dir or config.json file
```

Example:
: ```pycon
  >>> import os
  >>> import phanterpwa
  >>> from phanterpwa.configer import ProjectConfig
  >>> test_folder = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(phanterpwa.__file__), "tests", "test_configer_path", "project01")
  >>> cfg = ProjectConfig(test_folder)
  ```

modules.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor
documentation:
Title: XmlConstructor

Author: PhanterJR<junior.conex@gmail.com>

License: MIT

Coding: utf-8

XmlConstructor is for writing html using the python language. With it you can minify, save to file, sanitize,
translate (i18n), generate python code from html, etc.

classes.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.id
documentation:
:GET:

    Get element id

    Example:
        >>> UL = XmlConstructor.tagger("div")
        >>> instanceUL = UL()
        >>> other_instanceUL = UL()
        >>> print(instanceUL.id)
        32344567
        >>> print(other_instanceUL.id)
        234678454

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.introspect
documentation:
:GET:

    This property returns a representation of the instance, with
    the class, id, and tag information.

    Example:
        >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import DIV
        >>> instanceDIV = DIV()
        >>> print(instanceDIV.introspect)
        <class 'phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.tagger.<locals>.TAGGER'> {id: 46269904, tag: div}

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.all_instances
documentation:
:GET:

    This property returns a dict with all instances created
    The key is the id and the value is the instance

    Example:
        >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import DIV, SPAN
        >>> instanceDIV = DIV(SPAN() * 3)
        >>> print(instanceDIV.all_instances)
        {2384912: <class 'phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.tagger.<locals>.TAGGER'> {id: 2384912, tag: span},
        47221040: <class 'phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.tagger.<locals>.TAGGER'> {id: 47221040, tag: span},
        47221136: <class 'phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.tagger.<locals>.TAGGER'> {id: 47221136, tag: span},
        2387280: <class 'phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.tagger.<locals>.TAGGER'> {id: 2387280, tag: div}

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.tag_list
documentation:
:GET:

    list all tags used in instance creation

    Example:
        >>> DIV = XmlConstructor.tagger("div")
        >>> SPAN = XmlConstructor.tagger("span")
        >>> instanceDIV = DIV(SPAN() * 3)
        >>> print(instanceDIV.tag_list)
        ['span', 'div']

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.was_copied
documentation:
When we add an instance to an element automatically it will be the parent, if we add the same instance to another element, it will already have a parent, so an instance must only have one parent, so in those cases a copy of the instance is created to add a parent different, the list of copies is stored in this property. :GET: Returns a set with all instances copied Example: >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import DIV, SPAN >>> sourceInstance = SPAN("Is this the same instance? get id") >>> containerDIV = DIV(sourceInstance, sourceInstance, sourceInstance) >>> print(containerDIV)
Is this the same instance? get idIs this the same instance? get idIs this the same instance? get id
>>> sourceInstance.content = "changed!" >>> print(containerDIV)
changed!Is this the same instance? get idIs this the same instance? get id
If you want to access the copies just use this property (was_copied): >>> for x in sourceInstance.was_copied: x.content = "changed!" >>> print(containerDIV)
changed!changed!changed!

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.root_parent
documentation:
This property returns the main instance (root), in which the other instances are introduced. :GET: returns the main instance (root) Example: >>> DIV = XmlConstructor.tagger("div") >>> SPAN = XmlConstructor.tagger("span") >>> child_instance = DIV("I am a child") >>> root_parent = DIV(SPAN(child_instance), SPAN()) >>> print(root_parent) # the root_parent
I am a child
>>> print(child_instance) # the child_instance
I am a child
>>> print(child_instance.parent) # parent of child_instance
I am a child
 >>> print(child_instance.root_parent) # The root instance called from child instance
I am a child

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.parent
documentation:
This property returns the parent of the instance. If the instance is main element (root), it returns None :GET: Returns the parent of the instance. Example: >>> DIV = XmlConstructor.tagger("div") >>> SPAN = XmlConstructor.tagger("span") >>> child_instance = DIV("I am a child") >>> root_parent = DIV(SPAN(child_instance), SPAN()) >>> print(root_parent) # the root_parent
I am a child
>>> print(child_instance) # the child_instance
I am a child
>>> print(child_instance.parent) # parent of child_instance (SPAN)
I am a child

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.tag_begin
documentation:
:GET: Get begin tag. Example: >>> DIV = XmlConstructor.tagger('div', False) >>> instance_element = DIV() >>> print(instance_element)
>>> instance_element.attributes = {"_class": "my_class", "_id": "my_id"} >>> print(instance_element.tag_begin)

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.tag_end
documentation: :GET: Get end tag. Example: >>> DIV = XmlConstructor.tagger('div', False) >>> instance_element = DIV() >>> instance_element.attributes = {"_class": "my_class", "_id": "my_id"} >>> print(instance_element.tag_end) '' >>> HR = XmlConstructor.tagger('hr', True) >>> instance_element = HR() >>> print(instance_element.tag_end) >>>

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.xml_content
documentation:
With the xml_content property you can view the generated xml of the elements that are in the content of the main element. :GET: gets xml generated from content elements Example: >>> DIV = XmlConstructor.tagger("div") >>> SPAN = XmlConstructor.tagger("span") >>> html = DIV(SPAN(DIV()), SPAN()) >>> print(html)
>>> print(html.xml_content) # Only the xml of the elements contained in the root element

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.xml_attributes
documentation:
With the xml_attributes property you can view the generated
 string of the attribute from element.

:GET:

    gets string generated from element

    Example:
        >>> DIV = XmlConstructor.tagger("div")
        >>> instanceDIV = DIV(_id="my_id", _class="my_class")
        >>> print(instanceDIV.xml_attributes)
        id="my_id" class="my_class"

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.xml_humanized_content
documentation:
With the xml_humanized_content property you can view the generated string of the content from element indented. :GET: gets string indented of the content from element Example: >>> DIV = XmlConstructor.tagger("div") >>> SPAN = XmlConstructor.tagger("div") >>> instanceDIV = DIV(SPAN("My content"), DIV("Other content")) >>> print(instanceDIV.xml_humanized_content) My content
Other content

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.all_children
documentation: :GET: Returns a dict with all direct and indirect children, the keys are the index and the value is the instance. This option only affects the instance to which it is applied. Example: >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import (DIV, SPAN, A, BUTTON) >>> instanceDIV = DIV(SPAN(A(BUTTON("the direct parent is button")))) >>> for x in instanceDIV.all_children: print('index:', x, '- xml:', instanceDIV.all_children[x]) index: [0] - xml: index: [0][0] - xml: index: [0][0][0] - xml: index: [0][0][0][0] - xml: the direct parent is button

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.tag
documentation:
:GET: Get the tag name of instance Example: >>> DIV = XmlConstructor.tagger('div') >>> instanceDIV = DIV() >>> print(instanceDIV.tag) div :SET: Set the tag of element :Parameter: - **tag_name** - Name of tag Example: >>> DIV = XmlConstructor.tagger('div') >>> my_instance = DIV() >>> print(my_instance)
>>> my_instance.tag = 'button' >>> print(my_instance)

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.void
documentation:
There are elements without content that does not have an end tag, they are the void elements. With this property it is possible to set this. This property can be set by using the tagger method. :GET: Get the void parameter Example: >>> my_instance = XmlConstructor.tagger("button")() # The default is False >>> print(my_instance.void) False :SET: Set a void parameter. So by setting the void parameter False, automatically the close_void parameter will also be set to False. :Parameter: - **value** - True or False to set if element is void or not. Example (True) using the tagger method: >>> my_instance = XmlConstructor.tagger("br", void=True)() >>> print(my_instance)
>>> my_instance = XmlConstructor.tagger("hr", True)() >>> print(my_instance)
Example (False) using the tagger method: >>> my_instance = XmlConstructor.tagger("div")() >>> print(my_instance)
>>> my_instance = XmlConstructor.tagger("h1", False) >>> print(my_instance("My title"))

My title

:Example change instance: >>> my_instance = XmlConstructor.tagger("mypersonal_tag")() >>> print(my_instance) >>> my_instance.void = True >>> print(my_instance) >>> my_instance.void = False >>> print(my_instance)

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.close_void
documentation:
In the void elements of html5 closing or not the tag is optional, if you are writing a html legacy (xhtml) with this property you can configure the closing of the tag. :GET: Get the close_void parameter Example: >>> my_instance = XmlConstructor.tagger("meta", void=True)(_charset="utf-8") >>> print(my_instance) >>> print(my_instance.close_void) False :SET: Set a close_void parameter The change is only effective if the void parameter is also True. :Parameter: - **value** - True or False to set if the void element is closed or not Example (False) using the tagger method: >>> my_instance = XmlConstructor.tagger("hr", void=True) # The default is False >>> print(my_instance().close_void) False >>> print(my_instance())
Example (True) using the tagger method: >>> my_instance = XmlConstructor.tagger("div", void=False)() >>> print(my_instance.close_void) # Is False because the void is False False >>> print(my_instance())
>>> my_instance = XmlConstructor.tagger("br", void=True)() >>> my_instance.close_void = True >>> print(my_instance)
>>> my_instance2 = XmlConstructor.tagger("hr", True, True)() >>> my_instance.attributes = {"_class": "has_class"} >>> print(my_instance)
Example change instance: >>> my_instance = XmlConstructor.tagger("hr")() >>> print(my_instance)
>>> my_instance.close_void = True >>> print(my_instance) # The change is only effective if the void parameter is also True
>>> my_instance.void = False >>> print(my_instance)
>>> my_instance.close_void = False >>> print(my_instance)

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.minify
documentation:
The xml method is called internally when the instance is converted to string (__str__) (when we print for example), this property changes the xml format generated by the xml method, the default is True, when False the generated xml is indented. By changing an attribute of the Class the change reflects in all instances. :GET: Get the minify parameter. (default is True) :SET: Set the minify parameter. :Parameter: - **value** - True or False Example: >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import DIV >>> instanceDIV = DIV(DIV("content", _id="id_child"), _id="id_parent", _class="my_div") >>> otherInstanceDIV = DIV("other_instance", _id="id_other") >>> print(otherInstanceDIV.minify) True >>> print(instanceDIV.minify) True >>> print(instanceDIV)
content
>>> print(otherInstanceDIV)
other_instance
>>> instanceDIV.minify = False # change minify from instanceDIV >>> print(otherInstanceDIV.minify) # reflects in all instances False >>> print(instanceDIV)
content
>>> print(otherInstanceDIV)
other_instance

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.indent_size
documentation:
:GET: Get current indentation of the class Example: >>> DIV = XmlConstructor.tagger("div") >>> DIV.indent_size 2 :SET: Set the indentation of the xml method when the minification (minify attribute) is False and also the indentation of the humanize method. This property uses a classmethod, so a change reflects on all instances. :Parameter: - **size** - indentation size (2 is default, type int). Example: >>> DIV = XmlConstructor.tagger("div") >>> instanceDIV = DIV("my content", _id="my_id") >>> otherInstance = DIV("my other content", _id="my_other_id") >>> print(instanceDIV)
my content
>>> print(otherInstance)
my other content
>>> instanceDIV.minify = False # minify is classmethod, this reflect all instances >>> print(instanceDIV)
my content
>>> print(otherInstance)
my other content
>>> otherInstance.indent_size = 4 # indent_size is classmethod, this reflect all instances >>> print(instanceDIV)
my content
>>> instanceDIV.minify = True >>> print(otherInstance) # the indent_size is effective if the minify parameter is False
my other content

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.escape_string
documentation:
By default every string escaped to prevent xss attacks, by setting this property to False strings will no longer be escaped. :GET: Get current setting (default is True) :SET: Set the setting to True or False :Parameter: - **value** - True or False Example: >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import SPAN, DIV >>> instanceSPAN = SPAN("I will destroy you.") >>> print(instanceSPAN) <evil>I will destroy you.</evil> >>> instanceSPAN.escape_string=False >>> print(instanceSPAN) I will destroy you. >>> print(DIV("", instanceSPAN, ""))
<other_evil>I will destroy you.</other_evil>

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.alternative_tag
documentation:
the alternative tag is used in the source_code and childern_indexes
methods on elements that do not have a tag.
In some cases it is necessary to have containers without a tag,
but a tag is necessary for the methods mentioned (source_code and children_indexes).

:GET:

    Get alternative tag. By default the tag name is used if no new name is defined.
    If in the metaclass definition the tag is not assigned, the name "empty_tag" is used by default

    Example:

        >>> DIV = XmlConstructor.tagger('div')
        >>> instanceDIV = DIV()
        >>> print(instanceDIV.alternative_tag)
        div
        >>> METACLASS_WITHOUT_TAG = XmlConstructor.tagger('')
        >>> instanceEmptyTag = METACLASS_WITHOUT_TAG("content")
        >>> print(instanceEmptyTag.alternative_tag)
        empty_tag
        >>> print(instanceEmptyTag.source_code())
        EMPTY_TAG(
            'content'
        )
        >>> print(instanceEmptyTag.children_indexes())
        [ROOT_PARENT]<empty_tag> {}
            [0]"content"
        }

:SET:

    Set the alternative tag of element

    :Parameter:
        - **tag_name** - Name of tag (type: str)

    Example:
        >>> METACLASS_WITHOUT_TAG = XmlConstructor.tagger('')
        >>> instanceEmptyTag = METACLASS_WITHOUT_TAG("content")
        >>> print(instanceEmptyTag.alternative_tag)
        empty_tag
        >>> print(instanceEmptyTag.source_code())
        EMPTY_TAG(
            'content'
        )
        >>> print(instanceEmptyTag.children_indexes())
        [ROOT_PARENT]<empty_tag> {}
            [0]"content"
        }
        >>> instanceEmptyTag.alternative_tag = "new_alternative_name_to_tag"
        >>> print(instanceEmptyTag.source_code())
        NEW_ALTERNATIVE_NAME_TO_TAG(
            'content'
        )
        >>> print(instanceEmptyTag.children_indexes())
        [ROOT_PARENT]<new_alternative_name_to_tag> {}
            [0]"content"
        }

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.before_xml
documentation:
In some cases it is necessary to add a string before the generated xml, such as opening a comment, this is possible with this property :GET: Get before_xml :SET: Set a string to be inserted before the generated xml. :Parameter: - **value** - string to be insert Examples: >>> HTML = XmlConstructor.tagger('html') >>> P = XmlConstructor.tagger('p') >>> my_comment = P("My comment") >>> print(my_comment)

My comment

>>> print(my_comment.before_xml) # The default is empty >>> my_comment.before_xml = '' # Set a value in before_xml >>> my_comment.before_xml >>> print(my_comment)

My comment

# The value to be inserted before xml >>> is_html5 = HTML() >>> is_html5.before_xml = "" >>> print(is_html5)

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.after_xml
documentation:
In some cases it is necessary to add a string after the generated html, such as closing a comment, this is possible with this property. :GET: Get after_xml :SET: Set a string to be inserted after the generated xml. :Parameter: - **value** - string to be insert Examples: >>> HTML = XmlConstructor.tagger('html') >>> P = XmlConstructor.tagger('p') >>> is_comment = P("This is a comment") >>> is_not_comment = P("This is not a comment") >>> html = HTML(is_comment, is_not_comment) >>> print(html)

This is a comment

This is not a comment

>>> print(is_comment.after_xml) # The default is empty >>> is_comment.before_xml = '' # Set a value in after_xml >>> print(is_comment.after_xml) --> >>> print(is_comment) # The value to be inserted after xml >>> print(html)

This is not a comment

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.content
documentation:
The content property is one of the most important, with it you can add new elements in the root element. It is triggered when the element is instantiated, so elements can be added at creation time. You can also activate it later with the content property, the objects must be of type String, Other instances or insterable objects of the type List, Tuple and Set (You can also add a dict, but it will be treated by the attributes of the parent instance), within mentioned interfaces one can place all the objects already mentioned (nested). You can also add a function that returns any of the types mentioned above. :GET: Gets a FrozenConten(tuple) with all elements Example: >>> DIV = XmlConstructor.tagger("div") >>> Instance = DIV("This", "is", DIV("content")) # Set is triggered when the element is instantiated >>> print(Instance)
Thisis
content
>>> print(Instance.content) ('This', 'is', .TAGGER'> {id: 14811344, tag: div}) :SET: Adds new elements to the content. In it, String, Other instances (XmlConstructor), List, Tuple and Set are accepted. You can also add a dict, but it will be treated by the attributes of the parent instance. :Parameter: - **contents** - Elements to be added of type String, Other XmlConstructor instances or insterable objects of the type List, Tuple and Set (dict in special case). Example (String): >>> instanceDIV = XmlConstructor.tagger("div")() >>> instanceDIV.content = "my new content" >>> print(instanceDIV)
my new content
Example (XmlConstructor instance): >>> DIV = XmlConstructor.tagger("div") >>> STRONG = XmlConstructor.tagger("strong") >>> instanceDIV = DIV() >>> A = XmlConstructor.tagger("a", False) >>> instanceA = A("click ", STRONG("here"), _href="#my_url") >>> instanceDIV.content = instanceA >>> print(instanceDIV)
click here
Example using interable (List, Tuple, Set): >>> DIV = XmlConstructor.tagger("div") >>> SPAN = XmlConstructor.tagger("span") >>> HR = XmlConstructor.tagger("hr", True) >>> other_element = DIV([SPAN("Hello"), HR(), "World"]) >>> list_el = [other_element, other_element] >>> set_el = set(list_el) >>> tuple_el = (list_el, HR(), set_el, "final string") >>> new_element = DIV() >>> new_element.content = ['my_', 'content', SPAN('span, span, span'), tuple_el] >>> print(new_element.humanize())
my_ content span, span, span
Hello
World
Hello
World
Hello
World
final string
Example using function: >>> DIV = XmlConstructor.tagger("div") >>> SPAN = XmlConstructor.tagger("span") >>> instanceDIV.content = lambda: (SPAN("span * 3") * 3) + DIV("plus") >>> print(instanceDIV)
span * 3span * 3span * 3
plus
Example using dict (special case): >>> DIV = XmlConstructor.tagger("div") >>> instanceDIV1 = DIV("content") >>> instanceDIV1.content = dict(_id="my_id") >>> print(instanceDIV1)
>>> instanceDIV1.content = ["new_content", dict(_class="my_class")] >>> print(instanceDIV1)
new_content
>>> BR = XmlConstructor.tagger("br", True) >>> instanceDIV1.content = [set(["new_content", "new_content"]), [BR() * 2], {'_class': 'new_class'}] >>> instanceDIV1.close_void = True
new_content

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.attributes
documentation:
This property is also important, with it you can add the attributes in the instance of the element like classes, ids, styles, etc. It is stored in a dict, the keys must be compatible with the attributes of the HTML5 tags and the accepted values can be strings, lists of dictionaries. Keys must be started by underline (to avoid reserved python words, such as 'class' for example), keys that do not start with underline are stored in the Kargs attribute. :GET: Returns an instance of FrozenAttributes (dict) Example: >>> instanceDIV = XmlConstructor.tagger('div') >>> instanceDIV.attributes = {"_class": "my_class", "_id": "my_id"} >>> instanceDIV.attributes {"_class": "my_class", "_id": "my_id"} :SET: To add attributes it is recommended to use a dict, when a string is used a dict is created with the string as key and with the value True assigned. If the key values are added None or False, the attributes are ignored. If True is assigned, it will be an attribute with no content. With a function you can add values that would normally be ignored or modified. :Parameter: - **attrs** - Attributes to be added in the instance, can be a dict or string in special case. Example string: >>> BUTTON = XmlConstructor.tagger('button') >>> instanceBUTTON = BUTTON("Disabled Button") >>> instanceBUTTON.attributes = 'disabled' >>> print(instanceBUTTON) Example dict: Each key of the attribute must begin with underline (_) (because of the keyword class and id), keys without underline will store in Kargs attribute. >>> DIV = XmlConstructor.tagger('div') >>> instanceDIV = DIV("My Content", _class="this will be replaced", go_to_kargs="go_to_kargs") >>> instanceDIV.attributes = {"_class": "my_class", "_id": "my_id"} >>> print(instanceDIV)
My Content
Example dict, value None, False, True and Function: >>> DIV = XmlConstructor.tagger("div") >>> SPAN = XmlConstructor.tagger("span") >>> instanceDIV = DIV(_class="my_class", _underline="_underline", without_underline="without_underline") >>> print(instanceDIV)
>>> print(instanceDIV.attributes) {'_class': 'my_class', '_underline': '_underline'} >>> print(instanceDIV.kargs) {'without_underline': 'without_underline'} >>> instanceDIV.attributes = {"_teste": lambda: "false"} >>> print(instanceDIV)
>>> print(instanceDIV.attributes) {'_teste': at 0x02CD7858>} >>> instanceDIV = DIV(_id="my_class", _bye=None, _class=False) >>> print(instanceDIV)
>>> print(instanceDIV.attributes) {'_id': 'my_class'}

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.src_attr_dict
documentation:
This property affects the souce_code method, You can put 3 values,
None for automatic, False for disabled, and True for enabled.

:GET:

    Gets the current state of this property. None is default

    Example:
        >>> DIV = XmlConstructor.tagger("div")
        >>> instanceDIV = DIV()
        >>> instanceDIV.src_attr_dict
        None

:SET:

    Sets the value for this property

    :Parameter:
        - **value** - Sets here None, True or False

    In html, the elements attribute keys accept the minus
    character (-) in their name, in python no, we can
    define the attributes using arguments when
    instantiating, but this character can not be used, so
    we use a dict to get around the problem.

    Example None (default):
        >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import (DIV, HR, SPAN)
        >>> instanceDIV = DIV(HR(), SPAN(_class="the valid karg"))
        >>> instanceDIV.attributes = {"_invalid-attr-in-karg": "the_key_is_valis_in_html5"}
        >>> print(instanceDIV.source_code())
        DIV(
            HR(
            ),
            SPAN(
                _class='the valid karg'
            ),
            **{
                '_invalid-attr-in-karg': 'the_key_is_valis_in_html5'
            }
        )
        >>>

    Note that in the DIV element a dictionary was used to add
    the attributes and in the SPAN element an argument.

    Example True:
        >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import (DIV, HR, SPAN)
        >>> instanceDIV = DIV(HR(), SPAN(_class="the valid karg"))
        >>> instanceDIV.attributes = {"_invalid-attr-in-karg": "the_key_is_valis_in_html5"}
        >>> print(instanceDIV.source_code())
        DIV(
            HR(
            ),
            SPAN(
                **{
                    '_class': 'the valid karg'
                }
            ),
            **{
                '_invalid-attr-in-karg': 'the_key_is_valis_in_html5'
            }
        )

    Note that in all elements a dictionary was used to add the
    attributes.
    Example False (Not recommended):

        >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import (DIV, HR, SPAN)
        >>> instanceDIV = DIV(HR(), SPAN(_class="the valid karg"))
        >>> instanceDIV.attributes = {"_invalid-attr-in-karg": "the_key_is_valis_in_html5"}
        >>> print(instanceDIV.source_code())
        Traceback (most recent call last):
          ...
        ValueError: The argument name is invalid, given: _invalid-attr-in-karg

    It generates an error because the '_invalid-attr-in-karg' value
    can not be used as argument name in python.
    This option should only be used when you are sure that the keys
    of the html attributes are values that can be used as name
    identifier.

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.i18nInstance
documentation:
This property makes it possible to add an instance of the Translation object of the i18n module located in the phanterpwa package, this instance will be used to translate the strings. For more details see the documentation for the i18n method and the dictionary and tag_translation properties. :GET: Get the current phanterpwa.i18n.Translator instance :SET: Set the instance of object phanterpwa.i18n.Translator :Parameter: - **value** - Instance of phanterpwa.i18n.Translator object Example: >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import DIV, P >>> from phanterpwa.i18n import Translator >>> instanceDIV = DIV(P("Monkey")) >>> print(instanceDIV.humanize())

Monkey

>>> i18nMyInstance = Translator("path_of_languages", debug=True) >>> i18nMyInstance.translate("pt-BR", "Monkey", "Macaco") >>> i18nMyInstance.translate("fr", "Monkey", "Singe") >>> i18nMyInstance.translate("es", "Monkey", "Mono") >>> instanceDIV.i18nInstance = i18nMyInstance >>> print(instanceDIV.humanize()) # The default of tag_translarion is "span"

Monkey

>>> instanceDIV.dictionary = 'fr' # set specific dictionary >>> print(instanceDIV.humanize())

Singe

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.tag_translation
documentation:
With the i18n method you can perform two ways of translating strings, one of them makes more sense to be used on the server side and the other on the client side. With this property you enable the translation that is best for use on the client side, you define which element tag to use, the default is the 'span'. This property is effective only if there is a instance of the Translation object of the i18n module located in the phanterpwa package set on i18nInstance property. For more details read the documentation on the i18n method and dictionary and i18nInstance properties. :GET: get current tag. (default is "span") :SET: Set a new tag_translation. When you set a new valid tag name the dictionary property is automatically changed to None. :Parameter: - **value** - new tag name Example: >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import DIV, P >>> from phanterpwa.i18n import Translator >>> i18nMyInstance = Translator("path_of_languages", debug=True) >>> i18nMyInstance.translate("pt-BR", "Apple", "Maçã") >>> i18nMyInstance.translate("pt-BR", "Orange", "Laranja") >>> i18nMyInstance.translate("pt-PT", "Orange", "Laranja") >>> i18nMyInstance.translate("pt-PT", "Grape", "Uva") >>> instanceDIV = DIV(P("Apple", "Orange", "Grape", "Do not have translate")) >>> instanceDIV.i18nInstance = i18nMyInstance >>> print(instanceDIV.tag_translation) # tag_translation is "span" by default span >>> print(instanceDIV.humanize())

Apple Orange Grape Do not have translate

>>> instanceDIV.tag_translation = None >>> print(instanceDIV.humanize())

Apple Orange Grape Do not have translate

>>> instanceDIV.tag_translation = "i18n" >>> print(instanceDIV.humanize())

Apple Orange Grape Do not have translate

Note that for entries a span has been created with an attribute called phanterpwa-i18n, it has the list of dictionaries followed by the original entry and their translation, so on the client side just use a jquery plug-in to capture the json in the element attribute span and add to your content.

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.dictionary
documentation:
With the i18n method you can perform two ways of translating strings, one of them makes more sense to be used on the server side and the other on the client side. With this property you enable the translation that is best for use on the server side. The translation is direct. Here you define which dictionary will be used for translation. This property is effective only if there is a instance of the Translation object of the i18n module located in the phanterpwa package set in i18nInstance property. For more details read the documentation on the i18n method and tag_translation and i18nInstance properties. :GET: Set the current dictionary. (default is None) :SET: Set a dictionary. When setting a dictionary the tag_translation property automatically changes to None. :Parameter: - **value** - dictionary name Example: >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import DIV, P >>> from phanterpwa.i18n import Translator >>> instanceDIV = DIV(P("The book is on the table")) >>> print(instanceDIV.humanize())

The book is on the table

>>> i18nMyInstance = Translator("path_of_languages", debug=True) >>> i18nMyInstance.translate("pt-BR", "The book is on the table", "O livro está sobre a mesa") >>> i18nMyInstance.translate("fr", "The book is on the table", "Le livre est sur la table") >>> i18nMyInstance.translate("es", "The book is on the table", "El libro está sobre la mesa") >>> instanceDIV.i18nInstance = i18nMyInstance >>> print(instanceDIV.humanize()) # The default of tag_translarion is "span" and dictionary is None

The book is on the table

>>> instanceDIV.dictionary = 'fr' # set specific dictionary, tag_translation automatically changes to None >>> print(instanceDIV.humanize())

Le livre est sur la table

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.do_not_translate
documentation:
This property stores a list of strings that should be ignored in the translation. Read the documentation for the i18n method and tag_translate, dictionary, and i18nInstance properties for more details. This property is effective only if there is an instance of phanterpwa.i18n.Translator on i18nInstance and a valid parameter in the tag_translation or dictionary property. :GET: Get the current list string. (default is empty list []) :SET: Set the list string that should be ignored. :Parameter: - **value** - list string Example: >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import DIV, P >>> from phanterpwa.i18n import Translator >>> instanceDIV = DIV(P("The colors ", " Blue ", "and", " Green ", "are not translated.")) >>> print(instanceDIV.xml())

The colors Blue and Green are not translated.

>>> i18nMyInstance = Translator("path_of_languages", debug=True) >>> i18nMyInstance.translate("pt-BR", "The colors ", "As cores ") >>> i18nMyInstance.translate("pt-BR", " Blue ", " Azul ") >>> i18nMyInstance.translate("pt-BR", "and", "e") >>> i18nMyInstance.translate("pt-BR", " Green ", "Verde") >>> i18nMyInstance.translate("pt-BR", "are not translated.", "não são traduzidas.") >>> instanceDIV.i18nInstance = i18nMyInstance >>> print(instanceDIV.humanize())

The colors Blue and Green are not translated.

>>> instanceDIV.do_not_translate = [" Blue ", " Green "] >>> print(instanceDIV.humanize())

The colors Blue and Green are not translated.

>>> instanceDIV.dictionary = "pt-BR" >>> print(instanceDIV.humanize())

As cores Blue e Green não são traduzidas.

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.delimiters
documentation:
This property changes the delimiters of the formatter method. :GET: returns the current pair of delimiters. :SET: For a new pair of delimiters two strings are required in a list or tuple, the left string will be the left delimiter and vice versa. :Parameter: - **value** - Pair of de delimiters. Example: >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import DIV >>> instanceDIV = DIV("{{Change_this}} or not ", "[[change]]") >>> print(instanceDIV)
{{Change_this}} or not [[change]]
>>> instanceDIV.formatter(Change_this="Changed", change="hooooo nooooooo!") >>> print(instanceDIV)
Changed or not [[change]]
>>> print(instanceDIV.delimiters) ['{{', '}}'] >>> instanceDIV.delimiters = ["[[", "]]"] >>> print(instanceDIV.delimiters) ['[[', ']]'] >>> print(instanceDIV)
{{Change_this}} or not hooooo nooooooo!

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.formatter_list_keys
documentation:
:GET:

    This property lists all the keys in the generated xml that are within
    the delimiters (see the delimiters property documentation), keys can be
    converted to values with the formatter method (see the formatter
    documentation too)

    Example:
        >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import DIV, SPAN, A
        >>> instanceDIV = DIV(
                SPAN(
                    "This is value one: {{value-one}}",
                    "{this is ignored}"
                ),
                A("{{message_link}}", _href="{{attr_indefined}}"),
                SPAN("I want repeat: {{repeat}} {{repeat}} {{repeat}}.")
            )
        >>> print(instanceDIV.formatter_list_keys)
        ['value-one', 'message_link', 'attr_indefined', 'repeat']

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.sass_vars
documentation:
With this property you can view the variables contained in the sass file
applied in the sass_file method, you can also change the values of the
variables, as this change does not change the original file, but if a
target is detected in the sass_file method the change reflects in its
content.

```
GET

You can view the variables contained in the sass file applied in the
sass_file method.

SET

You can change the values of the variables contained in the sass
file applied in the sass_file method

Parameter

value - Dictionary with keys referencing variables and values changing current value.
```

Examples:

: Consider the sass file (my_sass.sass) with the following content:

  ```
  '''
  $COLOR: red
  $CONTAINERWIDTH: 60px
  $CONTAINERHEIGHT: 60px
  $BORDERRADIUS: 0
  .example_container
    *
      box-sizing: border-box
    .my_square
      display: inline-block
      position: relative
      width: $CONTAINERWIDTH
      height: $CONTAINERHEIGHT
      .other_square
        width: 100%
        height: 100%
        border-radius: $BORDERRADIUS

  '''
  ```

  ```pycon
  >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import DIV
  >>> instanceDIV = DIV(DIV(DIV(_class="other_square"), _class="my_square"), _class="example_container")
  >>> instanceDIV.sass_file("my_sass.sass")
  >>> print(instanceDIV.sass_vars)
  {'COLOR': 'red', 'CONTAINERWIDTH': '60px', 'CONTAINERHEIGHT': '60px', 'BORDERRADIUS': '0'}
  ```

Let's try modifying the COLOR and BORDERRADIUS variables.
The original file will not be modified.

> ```pycon
> >>> instanceDIV.sass_vars = {"COLOR": "orange", "BORDERRADIUS": "100%"}
> >>> print(instanceDIV.sass_vars)
> {'COLOR': 'orange', 'CONTAINERWIDTH': '60px', 'CONTAINERHEIGHT': '60px', 'BORDERRADIUS': '100%'}
> >>> print(instanceDIV.sass)
> $COLOR: orange
> $CONTAINERWIDTH: 60px
> $CONTAINERHEIGHT: 60px
> $BORDERRADIUS: 100%
> .example_container
>   *
>     box-sizing: border-box
>   .my_square
>     display: inline-block
>     position: relative
>     width: $CONTAINERWIDTH
>     height: $CONTAINERHEIGHT
>     .other_square
>       width: 100%
>       height: 100%
>       border-radius: $BORDERRADIUS
> >>>
> ```

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.sass_file

the associated sass. You can also change the values of the variables in
the code read from the sass file.

```
param file_name

sass file

param variables

Dictionary with the name of the variables and the new values. (Default is None)

param target

defines a file to which the generated content will be saved.
```

Example:

: Consider the sass file (my_sass.sass) with the following content:

  ```
  '''
  $COLOR: red

  $BORDERRADIUS: 0
  .example_container
    *
      box-sizing: border-box

        width: 100%

  '''
  ```

  ```pycon
  >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import DIV
  >>> instanceDIV = DIV(DIV(DIV(_class="other_square"), _class="my_square"), _class="example_container")
  >>> print(instanceDIV.sass_file("my_sass.sass"))
  $COLOR: red
  $CONTAINERWIDTH: 60px
  $CONTAINERHEIGHT: 60px
  $BORDERRADIUS: 0
  .example_container
    *

      .other_square
        width: 100%
        height: 100%
        border-radius: $BORDERRADIUS
  ```

Let's try modifying the COLOR and BORDERRADIUS variables.
The original file will not be modified.

> ```pycon
> >>> print(instanceDIV.sass_file("my_sass.sass", {"COLOR": "orange", "BORDERRADIUS": "100%"}))
> $COLOR: orange
> $CONTAINERWIDTH: 60px
> $CONTAINERHEIGHT: 60px
> $BORDERRADIUS: 100%
> .example_container
>   *

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.css
documentation:
After defining a sass file in the sass method, a css can be generated
with this method.

```
param minify

With minify active css will be minified.

param target

defines a file to which the generated content will be saved.
```

Example:

: Consider the sass file (my_sass.sass) with the following content:

  ```
  '''
  $COLOR: red
  $CONTAINERWIDTH: 60px
  $CONTAINERHEIGHT: 60px
  $BORDERRADIUS: 0
  .example_container
    *
      box-sizing: border-box
    .my_square
      display: inline-block
     position: relative
      width: $CONTAINERWIDTH
      height: $CONTAINERHEIGHT
      .other_square
        width: 100%
        height: 100%
        border-radius: $BORDERRADIUS
  '''
  ```

  ```pycon
  >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import DIV
  >>> instanceDIV = DIV(DIV(DIV(_class="other_square"), _class="my_square"), _class="example_container")
  >>> instanceDIV.sass_file("my_sass.sass")
  >>> print(instanceDIV.css(minify=False))
  .example_container * {
    box-sizing: border-box;
  }
  .example_container .my_square {
    display: inline-block;
    position: relative;
    width: 60px;
    height: 60px;
  }
  .example_container .my_square .other_square {
    width: 100%;
    height: 100%;
    border-radius: 100%;
  }
  >>>
  ```

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.formatter
documentation:
This method uses the format method used in strings, it is applied over the xml generated in the xml and humanized methods. The difference with the format method is that it accepts as its first argument a dictionary or a series of arguments with identifiers (kargs). ``` param value Dictionary with the identifier to be found with their respective values. param Kvalue Arguments with their respective identifiers and values. ``` Example: : ```pycon >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import DIV, SPAN, A >>> instanceDIV = DIV( SPAN( "This is value one: {{value-one}}", "{this is ignored}" ), A("{{message_link}}", _href="{{attr_indefined}}"), SPAN("I want repeat: {{repeat}} {{repeat}} {{repeat}}.") ) >>> print(instanceDIV.humanize())
This is value one: {{value-one}} {this is ignored} {{message_link}} I want repeat: {{repeat}} {{repeat}} {{repeat}}.
>>> instanceDIV.formatter({ "value-one": "1", "repeat": "Ha", "message_link": "click here!", "attr_indefined": "http://localhost", "this is ignored": "is it works?" }) >>> print(instanceDIV.humanize())
This is value one: 1 {this is ignored} click here! I want repeat: Ha Ha Ha.
``` The value-one name cannot be used as an argument identifier, so a dictionary was used to pass the arguments, it is possible to merge, see: > ```pycon > >>> instanceDIV.formatter( > {"value-one": "1", "this is ignored": "is it works?"}, > repeat="Ha", > message_link="click here!", > attr_indefined="http://localhost" > ) > >>> print(instanceDIV.humanize()) >
> > This is value one: 1 > {this is ignored} > > > click here! > > > I want repeat: Ha Ha Ha. > >
> >>> > ``` Or you can put it all in one dictionary and unpack with `**`. Note that in the first example the dictionary was passed as the first argument: > ```pycon > >>> instanceDIV.formatter(**{ > "value-one": "1", > "repeat": "Ha", > "message_link": "click here!", > "attr_indefined": "http://localhost", > "this is ignored": "is it works?" > }) > >>> print(instanceDIV.humanize()) >
> > This is value one: 1 > {this is ignored} > > > click here! > > > I want repeat: Ha Ha Ha. > >
> ```

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.children_indexes
documentation:
With the children_indexes method returns a string with the xml structure showing its respective indexes. With indexes you can easily access each element. Example: >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import (HTML, HEAD, META, TITLE, BODY, MAIN, XML, FOOTER) >>> instanceHTML = HTML( HEAD( META(_charset="utf-8"), TITLE("PhanterPWA")), BODY( MAIN( XML("xml instance not escape"), "this will escape" ), FOOTER("PhanterJR") ) ) XML (CONCATENATE too) object is omitted in xml generated. >>> print(instanceHTML.html(minify=False)) PhanterPWA
xml instance not escape <strong>this will escape<strong>
PhanterJR
>>> print(instanceHTML.children_indexes()) [ROOT_PARENT] {} [0] {} [0][0] {"_charset": "utf-8"} [0][1] {} [0][1][0]"PhanterPWA" [1]<body> {} [1][0]<main> {} [1][0][0]<xml> {} [1][0][0][0]"<strong>xml instance not escape</strong>" [1][0][1]"<strong>this will escape<strong>" [1][1]<footer> {} [1][1][0]"PhanterJR" Note that in the html method (xml() and humanize() method too) the XML (CONCATENATE too) object is omitted, but in the children_indexes method its index is shown.

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.source_code
documentation: With this method it is possible to generate static source code of the instance, it is useful to reverse engineer when used in conjunction with the HtmlToXmlConstructor object located in the reversexml module of the phanterpwa package. Another advantage is that the generated code is indented to facilitate and read. You can also add the necessary imports, using or not the helpers module of the phanterpwa package. It is important to read the documentation of the src_attr_dict property. ``` param add_imports add necessary imports in code This arguments will take effect if add_imports is true. param phanterpwa_helpers user phanter.helpers import. param instance_name The generated code is assigned to a variable with the name assigned here. param translate If True, the generated source_code strings will be translated. ``` Examples: : ```pycon >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import (DIV, HR, SPAN) >>> instanceDIV = DIV(XML("not escape"), HR(), SPAN("without xml?", _class="the valid karg")) >>> instanceDIV.attributes = {"_invalid-attr-in-karg": "the_key_is_valis_in_html5"} >>> print(instanceDIV.source_code()) DIV( XML( 'not escape' ), HR( ), SPAN( 'without xml?', _class='the valid karg' ), **{ '_invalid-attr-in-karg': 'the_key_is_valis_in_html5' } ) >>> print(instanceDIV.source_code(add_imports=True, phanterpwa_helpers=True)) from phanterpwa.helpers import ( XML, HR, SPAN, DIV ) . . html = DIV( XML( 'not escape' ), HR( ), SPAN( 'without xml?', _class='the valid karg' ), **{ '_invalid-attr-in-karg': 'the_key_is_valis_in_html5' } ) >>> instanceDIV.close_void = True >>> print(instanceDIV.source_code(add_imports=True, instance_name="MY_INSTANCE")) from phanterpwa.xmlconstructor import XmlConstructor from phanterpwa.third_parties.xss import XssCleaner . . class XML(XmlConstructor, XssCleaner): def __init__(self, content, sanitize=False, permitted_tags=[ 'a', 'b', 'blockquote', 'br/', 'i', 'li', 'ol', 'ul', 'p', 'cite', 'code', 'pre', 'img/', ], allowed_attributes={ 'a': ['href', 'title'], 'img': ['src', 'alt'], 'blockquote': ['type'] }): XmlConstructor.__init__(self, "", False, content) XssCleaner.__init__(self) self.alternative_tag = "xml" self.sanitize = sanitize self.permitted_tags = permitted_tags self.allowed_attributes = allowed_attributes self.strip_disallowed = False self.escape_string = False . def xml(self): xml = "" if self.content: xml = self.xml_content if self.sanitize: xml = "".join([self.before_xml, xml, self.after_xml]) return self.strip(xml) xml = "".join([self.before_xml, xml, self.after_xml]) return xml . . HR = XmlConstructor.tagger('hr', True) SPAN = XmlConstructor.tagger('span') DIV = XmlConstructor.tagger('div') . . MY_INSTANCE = DIV( XML( 'not escape' ), HR( ), SPAN( 'without xml?', _class='the valid karg' ), **{ '_invalid-attr-in-karg': 'the_key_is_valis_in_html5' } ) . MY_INSTANCE.close_void = True >>> ```

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.html
documentation:
With this method it is possible to generate an xml with different formats, with minimized indentation, with closed void elements without changing any other instance property. You can also write the generated xml to a file. ``` param minify If True the generated xml will be minified, if False will be indented. (default: True). param indent_size Only has effect with minify False, determines the size of the indentation. (default: 2). param close_void The xml of the void elements will be closed. (default: False). param translate With the translate True the translation will be applied (see more details in the i18n method). (default: False). param formatter Adds the Format String Syntax (str.format()) in the generated xml. (default: None). param i18nInstance Set phanterpwa.i18n.Translator instance to translate, dictionary or tag_translation must be valid argument. param tag_translation Set the tag used on translation on client side. param dictionary Specifics the dictionary that be used. Need a valid parameter on i18nTranslate. param do_not_translate List with the Words that be ignored in translation. param escape_string The strings aren't be scaped if escape_string is False. True is default. param file Name of the file that will be written to the generated xml. (default: None). param encoding It only takes effect if the file argument is other than None, here determines the encoding used in the xml script in the file. ``` Example: : ```pycon >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import (DIV, BR, HR, SPAN) >>> instanceDIV = DIV(DIV(BR(), HR(), SPAN()), _class="my_class", _underline="_underline", without_underline="without_underline") >>> # The method html is not affected by these attributes >>> instanceDIV.minify = False >>> instanceDIV.indent_size = 4 >>> instanceDIV.close_void = True >>> print(instanceDIV.html()) # default: minify=True, indent_size=2, close_void=False

>>> print(instanceDIV.html(False, 8)) # minify=False, indent_size=8, close_void=False(default)

>>> print(instanceDIV.html(minify=False, close_void=True)) # minify=False, indent_size=4, close_void=False(default)

``` The method humanize is affected by the indent_size and close_void attribute > ```pycon > >>> print(instanceDIV.humanize()) >
>
>
>
> > >
>
> ``` The method xml is affected by the minify, indent_size (when instance.minify=True) and close_void attribute. > ```pycon > >>> instanceDIV.minify = True > >>> print(instanceDIV.xml()) >

> >>> instanceDIV.minify = False > >>> print(instanceDIV.xml()) >
>
>
>
> > >
>
> ```

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.humanize
documentation:
The humanize method returns the xml in indented format for easier reading by humans, it is affected by the close_void and indent_size attributes of the class. Example: >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import (DIV, BR, HR, SPAN) >>> instanceDIV = DIV( DIV( BR(), HR(), SPAN() ), _class="my_class", _underline="_underline", without_underline="without_underline" ) >>> print(instanceDIV.humanize())

>>> instanceDIV.close_void = True >>> print(instanceDIV.humanize())

>>> instanceDIV.indent_size = 4 >>> print(instanceDIV.humanize())

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.xml
documentation:
With this method it is possible to generate a xml of the instance. The format of the generated xml depends on the void_close, minify, and indent_size (indent size will only take effect if minify for False) properties that change class attributes XmlConstructor. Example: >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import (DIV, BR, HR, SPAN) >>> instanceDIV = DIV( DIV( BR(), HR(), SPAN() ), _class="my_class", _underline="_underline" ) >>> # The method html is not affected by these attributes >>> instanceDIV.indent_size = 4 >>> instanceDIV.close_void = True >>> print(instanceDIV.xml())

>>> instanceDIV.minify = False >>> print(instanceDIV.xml())

>>> instanceDIV.indent_size = 8 >>> instanceDIV.close_void = False >>> print(instanceDIV.xml())

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.json
documentation:
In some cases, it is necessary to pass the generated XML from the instance to an argument from some javascript library, such as jquery for example, so this method converts xml (multiline) to json string (quotted). ``` param kargs to kargs json.dumps. ``` Example: : ```pycon >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import (DIV, HR, SPAN, SCRIPT) >>> instanceDIV = DIV( ... DIV("multline\nline01\nline02"), ... SPAN("it's red", _style="color:red;"), ... HR() ... ) >>> print(instanceDIV)
multline line01 line02
it's red
>>> print(instanceDIV.json()) "
multline\nline01\nline02
it's red
" >>> instanceSCRIPT = SCRIPT('$("#my_id").html({0});'.format(instanceDIV.json())) >>> print(instanceSCRIPT) ```

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.search
documentation:
With this method you can find instances contained in the main instance by giving it some parameters such as: string, dict, other instances and integers. Each type will be treated differently, as we will see below. \:param search: Search Parameter, must be string, XmlConstructor, dict, int, list, set or tuple. It will always return a list with the result, if not found it will be empty. Consider the following instances below: > ```pycon > >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import (DIV, SPAN, HR, HTML) > >>> instanceHR = HR(_class="one two") > >>> id_to_search = instanceHR.id > >>> print(id_to_search) > >>> sampleSEARCH = HTML( > ... DIV( > ... DIV( > ... "content", > ... _class="two", > ... _style="color: white; display : none;" > ... ), > ... SPAN( > ... "content", > ... HR( > ... _class="one two" > ... ) > ... ), > ... instanceHR, > ... DIV( > ... "long_content", > ... SPAN( > ... "long_content" > ... ), > ... _class="one", > ... _style="color: white" > ... ), > ... _style="display:none;" > ... ), > ... DIV("multiple") > ... ) > ``` Let's find using another instance, this way the method will look inside the contents of the main instance for all instances that generate the same xml. > ```pycon > >>> search = SPAN("long_content") > >>> result = sampleSEARCH.search(search) > >>> print("located", len(result)) > located 1 > >>> for x in result: > ... print("instance:", x.introspect) > ... print("xml:", x) > instance: .TAGGER'> {id: 47216592, tag: span} > xml: long_content > ``` Let's search now using a string, so the method will search within content strings that have at least one occurrence returning the parent instance that contains the string. > ```pycon > >>> search = "long_content" > >>> result = sampleSEARCH.search(search) > >>> print("located", len(result)) > located 2 > >>> for x in result: > ... print("It has the string 'long_content'?", x) > It has the string 'long_content'?
long_contentlong_content
> It has the string 'long_content'? long_content > >>> search = "content" > >>> result = sampleSEARCH.search(search) > >>> print("located", len(result)) > located 4 > >>> for x in result: > ... print("It has the string 'content'?", x) > It has the string 'content'?
long_contentlong_content
> It has the string 'content'? content
 > It has the string 'content'?
content
> It has the string 'content'? long_content > ``` Let's search now using a dict, so the method will look for all instances that have the same attributes as searched, the values must be the same except for the \_class and \_style attributes where just match a string in their content. > ```pycon > >>> result = sampleSEARCH.search({'_style':" display: none;"}) > >>> print("located", len(result)) > located 2 > >>> for x in result: > ... print("xml:", x) > xml:
content
> xml:
content
content
long_contentlong_content
> >>> result = sampleSEARCH.search({'_style':" display: none;", '_class': 'two'}) > >>> print("located", len(result)) > located 1 > >>> for x in result: > ... print("xml:", x) > xml:
content
> >>> result = sampleSEARCH.search({'_class': 'two'}) > >>> print("located", len(result)) > located 3 > >>> for x in result: > ... print("xml:", x) > xml:
> xml:
> xml:
content
> >>> result = sampleSEARCH.search({'_class': 'two one'}) > >>> print("located", len(result)) > located 2 > >>> for x in result: > ... print("xml:", x) > xml:
> xml:
> ``` When we add an integer the method will look for the id. > ```pycon > >>> result = sampleSEARCH.search(id_to_search) > >>> print("located", len(result)) > located 1 > >>> for x in result: > ... print("xml:", x) > xml:
> ``` When we add a list, a set or a tuple, it will do multiple searches returning a list with all the instances found. > ```pycon > >>> result = sampleSEARCH.search([ > ... id_to_search, SPAN("long_content"), "multiple" > ... ]) > >>> print("located", len(result)) > located 3 > >>> for x in result: > ... print("xml:", x) > xml:
multiple
> xml: long_content > xml:
> ```

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.i18n
documentation:
With this method it is possible to translate the strings contained in the main instance and its children, it uses the Translator class of the module i18n of the phanterpwa package, it replaces the strings with the corresponding translation. You can perform two ways of translating strings, one of them makes more sense to be used on the server side and the other on the client side. One of the ways of translation, it is translated directly using a specific dictionary, the xml generated in the xml and humanize methods have the strings found in the dictionary translated to use this method you must have an instance of the phanterpwa.i18n.Translator object in the i18nInstance argument and a valid dictionary specified in the dictionary argument. In the other way, it creates an element with a tag specified in the tag_translation argument and with an attribute called "phanterpwa_i18n" in which a dictionary with all dictionaries with that specific translation is stored, on the client side you can use a Jquery plug-in, example: `$("#id_parent_element").phanterpwa_i18n();` This plug-in from phanterpwa.js replace the element content with the attribute value of a specific dictionary, which may even be the browser language. Remember that nothing prevents the server side using this common method on the client side or vice versa. Args: > ``` > param i18nInstance > > phanterpwa.i18n.Translator instance. > > param dictionary > > A dictionary disponible on phanterpwa.i18n.Translator. > When setting a dictionary the tag_translation automatically > changes to None. > > param tag_translation > > New tag_translation name. When you set a new > valid tag name the dictionary is automatically changed to None. > > param do_not_translate > > list of strings that should be ignored in the > translation > ``` Example: : ```pycon >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import DIV, P >>> from phanterpwa.i18n import Translator >>> instanceDIV = DIV( "Can I use formatter method? {response}", P("Monkey", ". Don't have translation this and not has translation of ", "Camel", " in Spanish"), P("Do not exist translation"), P("Apple", "Orange", "Grape", "Do not have translate"), P("The book is on the table", " is ignored") ) >>> print(instanceDIV.humanize())
Can I use formatter method? {response}

Monkey . Don't have translation this and not has translation of Camel in Spanish

Do not exist translation

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.insert

Add a new element in specific position of the content. \:param position: position of the content ``` Parameter value - Element to be added ``` Example: : ```pycon >>> DIV = XmlConstructor.tagger("div") >>> my_instance = DIV() >>> print(my_instance)

content0

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.replace
documentation:
Replace a element in specific position in the content. \:param position: position of the element in the content to be replaced ``` Parameter value - Element to be added ``` Example: : ```pycon >>> DIV = XmlConstructor.tagger("div") >>> SPAN = XmlConstructor.tagger("span") >>> HR = XmlConstructor.tagger("hr", True) >>> my_instance = DIV(SPAN(), HR(), DIV()) >>> print(my_instance)
>>> my_instance.replace(2, SPAN()) >>> print(my_instance)
```

methods.name: phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor.tagger
documentation:
Using the tagger method you can create a metaclass of class
XmlConstructor

XmlConstructor is for writing html using the python language. When instantiating we use the following parameters ``` param tag Name of tag. Example: div, img, br param void If True, the tag does not has content param content Content of element. exemple: >>> XmlConstructor.tagger("this is", " my content") param attributes Element attributes. Each key of the attribute must begin with underline (_) (because of the keyword class and id), keys without underline will store on yourInstance.kargs ``` Examples: : ```pycon >>> class DIV(XmlConstructor): def __init__(self, *content, **attributes): XmlConstructor.__init__(self, "div" False, *content, **attributes) >>> print(DIV())
``` Or you can (recommended). Using the tagger method you can create a metaclass of class XmlConstructor. > ```pycon > >>> DIV = XmlConstructor.tagger("div") > >>> print(DIV()) >
> >>> print(DIV("My content", _class="my_atribute_class")) >
My content
> ``` IMPORTANT, Use the xml (or html) method to capture the results you can see in the print command > ```pycon > >>> DIV = XmlConstructor.tagger("div") > >>> print(DIV("My content", _class="my_atribute_class")) >
My content
> >>> DIV("My content", _class="my_atribute_class").xml() > '
My content
' > >>> instanceDIV = DIV("My content", _class="my_atribute_class") > >>> instanceDIV.xml() > '
My content
' > >>> print(instanceDIV) >
My content
> >>> new_instance = DIV("line0", "\nline1") > >>> new_instance.xml() > '
line0\nline1
' > >>> print(new_instance) # the print command convert "\n" to breaklines >
line0 > line1
> ``` We already have tags ready to use, no need to reinvent the wheel. In the package module helpers of the phanterpwa package contains all tags used for html5, just import with. > ```pycon > >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import * > ``` Or just what you want. > ```pycon > >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import (HTML, HEAD, BODY, DIV, SPAN) > ``` The complete list can be obtained with. > ```pycon > >>> from phanterpwa.helpers import ALL_TAGS > >>> print (ALL_TAGS) > ``` There is also a separate list by categories: NORMAL_TAGS, VOID_TAGS, and SPECIAL_TAGS. Examples with **void** True/False > ```pycon > >>> instenceBR = XmlConstructor.tagger("br", True)() >
> >>> print(XmlConstructor.tagger("hr", True)(_class="especial_hr")) >
> >>> print(XmlConstructor.tagger("img", True)(_href="#my_url")) > > ``` if **void** is False, the tag will be close tag: > ```pycon > >>> print(XmlConstructor.tagger("div")()) >
> >>> H1 = XmlConstructor.tagger("h1", False, False) > >>> print(H1("My title")) >

My title

> >>> BUTTON = XmlConstructor.tagger("button", False, False) > >>> print(BUTTON("my_content", _class="my_class")) > > ``` BEST PRACTICE: To help in use, use the classmethod tagger, in this way you can suppress some parameters. Using the tagger method you can create a metaclass of class XmlConstructor, see: > ```pycon > >>> DIV = XmlConstructor.tagger("div") > >>> IMG = XmlConstructor.tagger("img", True) > >>> BR = XmlConstructor.tagger("br", True) > >>> print(DIV()) >
> >>> print(DIV("this", "is", "join", " its no. ", "evil")) >
thisisjoin its no. <tag_evil>evil</tag_evil>
> >>> print(IMG(_class="images")) > > >>> print(BR()) >
> >>> HR = XmlConstructor.tagger("hr", True) > >>> instanceBR = BR() > >>> print(instanceBR) >
> >>> instanceBR.close_void = True # If True, the tag of void elements are closed with "/". > >>> print(instanceBR) >
> >>> instanceHR = HR(_class="especial_hr") > >>> print(instanceHR) # This close_void uses a classmethod, so a change reflects on all instances >
> >>> instanceBR.close_void = False > >>> print(instanceHR) # The close_void is False >
> >>> print(IMG(_href="#my_url")) > > ```

functions.name: phanterpwa.reversexml.force_minify_string_content
documentation:
Forces the minification of the string content by removing multiple empty spaces, line breaks, tabs replacing
with a single space.

```
param target

XmlConstructor (or HtmlToXmlConstructor) instance
```

Example:
: ```pycon
  >>> from phanterpwa.reversexml import (HtmlToXmlConstructor, force_minify_string_content)
  >>> sample = '''
  ... <html>
  ...     <head>
  ...         <meta charset="utf-8">
  ...     </head>
  ...     <body>
  ...         Forces      the       minification
  ...         of the     string content       by
  ...         removing       multiple     empty
  ...         spaces,      line       breaks,
  ...         tabs      replacing     with a
  ...         single    space.
  ...     </body>
  ... </html>
  ... '''
  >>> html_to_xmlconstructor_instance = HtmlToXmlConstructor(sample)
  >>> print(html_to_xmlconstructor_instance.xml())
  <html>
      <head>
          <meta charset="utf-8">
      </head>
      <body>
          Forces      the       minification
          of the     string content       by
          removing       multiple     empty
          spaces,      line       breaks,
          tabs      replacing     with a
          single    space.
      </body>
  </html>
  >>> force_minify_string_content(html_to_xmlconstructor_instance)
  >>> print(html_to_xmlconstructor_instance.xml())
  <html><head><meta charset="utf-8"></head><body> Forces the minification of the string content by removing multiple empty spaces, line breaks, tabs replacing with a single space. </body></html>
  >>> print(html_to_xmlconstructor_instance.xmlconstructor_code())
  HTML(
      HEAD(
          META(
              _charset='utf-8'
          )
      ),
      BODY(
          ' Forces the minification of the string content by removing multiple empty spaces, line breaks, tabs replacing with a single space. '
      )
  )
  ```

methods.name: phanterpwa.reversexml.HtmlToXmlConstructor.xmlconstructor_code
documentation:
Returns the generated source code, does the same as the source_code method with the difference of omit the CONCATENATE root tag when necessary. see the documentation for the source_code method of phanterpwa.xmlconstructor.XmlConstructor for more details. ``` param add_imports add necessary imports in code This arguments will take effect if add_imports is true. param phanterpwa_helpers user phanter.helpers import. param instance_name The generated code is assigned to a variable with the name assigned here. param translate If True, the generated source_code strings will be translated. ``` Example: : ```pycon >>> from phanterpwa.reversexml import HtmlToXmlConstructor >>> html_to_xmlconstructor_instance = HtmlToXmlConstructor("
start
my content
") >>> print(html_to_xmlconstructor_instance.xmlconstructor_code()) HTML( HEAD( META( _charset='UTF-8' ) ), BODY( NAV( BUTTOM( 'start' ), _class='navbar' ), MAIN( DIV( DIV( 'my content' ), _class='row' ), _id='my_content' ) ) ) >>> html_to_xmlconstructor_instance = HtmlToXmlConstructor("
content1
content2
") >>> print(html_to_xmlconstructor_instance.xmlconstructor_code()) CONCATENATE( DIV( 'content1', **{ '_data-dict': 'i am in dict', '_class': 'my_class' } ), DIV( 'content2', _class='my_class' ) ) ```

The XmlConstructor writes html, this object already does the opposite, with it it is possible with html to
write XmlConstructor.

At instantiation we have the following parameters

```
param strhtml

String with html code.

param filter_empty_content

Filters empty elements when True (False)

param filter_comments

Filter comments elements when True (True)
```
